image.title: DW
image.description: News, Analysis and Service from Germany and Europe - in 30 Languages

item.title: Film funding bill to support blockbusters made in Germany
item.description: The German government wants to attract more filmmakers and make the country a 'global player' in on-screen entertainment. A new bill would require media companies to make mandatory investments in the film industry.
item.dc:subject: Germany

item.title: WHO chief visits Ebola epicenter in eastern DR Congo
item.description: Addressing the public from the epicenter of the DRC Ebola outbreak, WHO chief Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus urged the residents to stay safe during burials of Ebola victims and called on the infected to seek care early.
item.dc:subject: News

item.title: Germany: Vaping becoming more popular among young people
item.description: A recent study shows that e-cigarettes are becoming increasingly popular among young people in Germany, with even elementary school kids picking up vapes. Health experts are alarmed.
item.dc:subject: Germany

item.title: What Hungary must do to receive EU funds frozen under Orban
item.description: Prime Minister Peter Magyar and European Commission President Ursula von der Leyen announced a deal that could free up over €16 billion in EU funds for Hungary. Now, Magyar's new government needs to deliver.
item.dc:subject: World

item.title: Laos: Five people rescued from flooded cave
item.description: Seven people who entered the cave last week were trapped inside by flash flooding that blocked their way. Five of them found some days ago have been freed after rescuers pumped water out of the cave system.
item.dc:subject: News

item.title: Russia recalls Armenia ambassador over EU ties
item.description: The recall comes a day after Putin warned Armenia against seeking EU membership and alluded to the "Ukrainian scenario." Armenia historically has strong ties with Russia but has pivoted toward the West in recent years.
item.dc:subject: News

item.title: Malta votes in snap parliamentary election
item.description: Voting is underway in Malta's parliamentary election. Opinion polls show Prime Minister Robert Abela on course for reelection in the shadow of the Middle East crisis.
item.dc:subject: News

item.title: Germany news: Alpine pass reopened after large protest
item.description: Thousands of people took part in the blockade of Brenner Pass, an Austrian road linking Germany and Italy, demanding less traffic. And a German minister is looking to crack down on screen time for kids. DW has more.
item.dc:subject: News

item.title: India news: Myanmar president arrives to boost ties
item.description: Myanmar President Min Aung Hlaing is on a five-day visit to India, where he will hold talks with Prime Minister Narendra Modi. Meanwhile, Karnataka is readying itself for a Cabinet change. Follow DW for more.
item.dc:subject: News

item.title: Iran war: US says ready to resume hostilities, Hegseth warns
item.description: The United States is "more than capable" of resuming the war with Iran, Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth said as a permanent ceasefire remains elusive. DW has more.
item.dc:subject: News

item.title: International aid cuts complicate Congo's Ebola epidemic
item.description: The scaling back of development aid has contributed to the inability to contain the Ebola outbreak in the Democratic Republic of Congo. German organizations are calling for a major spending boost to help stop the spread.
item.dc:subject: Science

item.title: Three Latvian mountaineers die on North America's tallest peak
item.description: Three climbers fell to their deaths near a perilous pass on Mount McKinley in the US's Denali National Park and Preserve. One mountaineer was rescued.
item.dc:subject: News

item.title: Shangri-La Dialogue: Hegseth says 'no state, including China,' must dominate Asia
item.description: US Defense Secretary of Pete Hegseth expressed alarm about China's military buildup in Asia. He also said the United States expects its allies and partners in Asia to increase defense spending to 3.5% of their GDP.
item.dc:subject: News

item.title: Top US and Cuban generals meet at Guantanamo Bay
item.description: Amid the deterioration of relations between the United States and Cuba, a top US general held a rare meeting with senior Cuban military officials at Guantanamo Bay.
item.dc:subject: News

item.title: US: Judge rules Trump name must be removed from Kennedy Center
item.description: Trump criticized the ruling, saying the judge "should be ashamed of himself," but will hand the venue over to Congress.
item.dc:subject: News

item.title: Ghana approves law criminalizing LGBTQ+ activities, advocacy
item.description: Ghana's Parliament passed one of the most repressive anti-LGBTQ+ laws in Africa. The law imposes prison terms on individuals who "promote" LGBTQ+ activities.
item.dc:subject: News

item.title: Romania's political crisis deepens
item.description: After Prime Minister Ilie Bolojan’s government fell in early May, Romania's moderate parties have struggled to chart a new course — opening the door for the far right to gain ground.
item.dc:subject: World

item.title: Romania: Russian drone strikes Galati apartment building
item.description: Romania accused Russia of a "grave and irresponsible escalation" after a Russian drone entered its airspace. German Chancellor Merz said "we need a strong NATO presence on the eastern flank" after the incident.
item.dc:subject: News

item.title: US judge halts Trump's 'anti-weaponization' fund
item.description: The billion-dollar "anti-weaponization" fund was meant to compensate people who Trump sees as victims of so-called "lawfare" by Joe Biden's administration.
item.dc:subject: News

item.title: EU unblocks €16 billion in Hungary assistance as Magyar promises reforms
item.description: Reforms under new Prime Minister Peter Magyar will enable the EU to free up billions in frozen assistance for Budapest. The EU's Ursula von der Leyen announced the news as Magyar visited Brussels.
item.dc:subject: News

item.title: What makes a vanity project? A history of building for power
item.description: As Donald Trump proposes costly redesigns for Washington, DC, the question arises: When do grand projects serve the public — and when do they serve the leader?
item.dc:subject: Culture

item.title: Kai Havertz: Arsenal and Germany's man for the big moment
item.description: Germany forward Kai Havertz is set to play in his second Champions League final. The Arsenal man is a match winner.
item.dc:subject: Sports

item.title: Germany deepens Taiwan ties amid China tensions
item.description: A German delegation, known as the Berlin-Taipei Parliamentary Friendship Group, recently visited Taiwan, drawing criticism from Beijing. Two lawmakers told DW the visit was important for expanding economic partnerships.
item.dc:subject: World

item.title: Pigeons' livers may enable magnetic navigation when the sun doesn't shine, study finds
item.description: A research team in Germany has published new data on how pigeons use magnetic fields as a navigation method when visibility is limited. A key part of the long-sought answer could lie in an unexpected organ, their livers.
item.dc:subject: News

item.title: Superbug crisis: How drugs that cure you end up in rivers, soils
item.description: Pharmaceuticals have been found in waterways almost everywhere in the world, fueling antibiotic-resistant superbugs that no longer respond to treatment. Is there a way out?
item.dc:subject: Environment

item.title: France moves to symbolically repeal slavery legislation
item.description: Members of the lower house of the French parliament unanimously voted to abolish a series of royal edicts known as the "Code noir" or Black Code. The bill stops short of some lawmakers' demands like possible reparations.
item.dc:subject: News

item.title: Suez, Panama canals charge for transit — why can't Hormuz?
item.description: Iran has been widely condemned for demanding up to $2 million for vessels to ship through the Strait of Hormuz. DW explores the reasons why Egypt can charge for the Suez Canal and Panama for its waterway, but Iran can't.
item.dc:subject: Business

item.title: Reiche brings energy sector ties to German Economy Ministry
item.description: Economy Minister Reiche is a controversial figure. Her plans for new gas-fired power plants, rolling back plans for more renewables in the building sector and scrapping solar subsidies have been met with criticism.
item.dc:subject: Germany

item.title: UK re-chalks Dorset's famed and feisty Cerne Abbas Giant
item.description: Britain's National Trust is working on replacing the chalk outline of the Cerne Abbas Giant in Dorset. The Saxon-era carving depicts a club-wielding man "still standing tall," as the charity puts it.
item.dc:subject: News

item.title: Why Europe is getting so hot
item.description: Europe is sweltering under extreme heat that has broken records and claimed lives. Why is the continent heating so fast?
item.dc:subject: World

item.title: New Ebola cases in Congo: What you need to know
item.description: The virus spreads from direct person-to-person contact. But here's what makes it especially lethal: it persists in corpses, and funeral practices often take place precisely when bodies are most infectious.
item.dc:subject: Science

item.title: Iran war: Oil shortages threaten global energy security
item.description: Strategic petroleum reserves are declining rapidly as countries release emergency crude supplies to cushion the economic impact of the Middle East conflict.
item.dc:subject: Business

item.title: FIFA World Cup: New York and New Jersey prosecutors probe 'sky-high' ticket prices
item.description: US prosecutors have subpoenaed FIFA over expensive World Cup tickets, saying the event is "not an invitation to exploit our residents and visitors."
item.dc:subject: News

item.title: Germany: No recovery in sight for the economy
item.description: The war in Iran has dashed hopes for economic growth. Germany's pension and healthcare systems are also feeling the strain.
item.dc:subject: Germany

item.title: EU enlargement: Is the bloc ready to welcome new members?
item.description: Brussels says the EU must grow, but resistance within the bloc remains strong, with some arguing that it must reform before enlarging. Montenegro remains the only country with a realistic chance of joining soon.
item.dc:subject: World

item.title: Turkey: Leadership dispute engulfs opposition CHP
item.description: The top figures in Turkey's main opposition party differ on how to resolve the leadership vacuum after a court annulled its 2023 primary. One wants the party delegates to pick a leader, the other wants members to vote.
item.dc:subject: News

item.title: Europe's energy problem isn't green power — it's storage
item.description: Solar and wind produce lots of energy — but not always at the right time. More battery storage could help Europe to stabilize prices and replace polluting fossil fuel energy, but roadblocks remain.
item.dc:subject: Environment

item.title: Germany eyes heat pumps as Iran war drives energy costs up
item.description: The war in the Middle East is threatening fossil fuel supplies and causing energy prices to rise in Germany. Could that drive a surge in demand for heat pumps?
item.dc:subject: Business

item.title: Spain: Police raid HQ of ruling PSOE Socialist party in graft probe
item.description: Police have searched the headquarters of Prime Minister Pedro Sanchez's PSOE party investigating possible illegal payments. Sanchez meanwhile said he was still standing by former PM Jose Luis Rodriguez Zapatero.
item.dc:subject: News

item.title: License to play: The new James Bond video game
item.description: James Bond is back, and he's as charismatic and daring as ever. The video game "007 First Light" tells the origin story of the British secret agent — and the stakes are high.
item.dc:subject: Culture

item.title: Germany: Alleged leftist militant found guilty of robbery
item.description: Daniela Klette, suspected to be one of the last remaining members of the left-wing terrorist group Red Army Faction, has been found guilty for her part in a series of robberies — but more serious charges await.
item.dc:subject: Germany

item.title: The German gym fighting the far right with MMA
item.description: The far right is increasingly using MMA to recruit new members. But a gym in Chemnitz, in the east of Germany, is fighting back with a martial arts program focused on diversity and empowerment.
item.dc:subject: Sports

item.title: How did Berlin's Jewish Hospital and hundreds of its patients survive the Holocaust?
item.description: When Red Army troops entered Berlin's Jewish Hospital in 1945, they were astonished to discover hundreds of Jews living just a couple of miles away from Hitler's bunker.
item.dc:subject: Germany

item.title: Ukraine: EU, Germany summon Russian envoys after Moscow tells diplomats to leave Kyiv
item.description: The EU and Germany both summoned Russian diplomats to protest attacks on Kyiv and orders for diplomats to leave the city. The UN secretary-general also rebuked Russia in a session on proliferating conflicts worldwide.
item.dc:subject: News

item.title: Ferrari unveils its first all-electric car, the 4-door Luce
item.description: The Italian supercar giant has released images of what will be both its first ever pure EV, and its first car that can seat five people. The Luce eyes a younger clientele and the increasingly competitive Chinese market.
item.dc:subject: News

item.title: In Nazi Germany, jazz was an act of defiance
item.description: The Nazis denounced jazz music as "degenerate art," despite its widespread popularity in Germany. Amid crackdowns on freedom of expression, groups of jazz-loving teenagers formed the Swing Youth in rebellion.
item.dc:subject: Culture

item.title: Putin offers debt relief to new recruits for Ukraine war
item.description: New recruits for Russia's war in Ukraine will have debts up to 10 million rubles canceled under a presidential decree. Another new law reportedly foresees the use of Russian armed forces to protect citizens abroad.
item.dc:subject: News

item.title: Belgium: Train collides with school bus, several dead
item.description: A train has crashed into a school bus near a town close to Brussels, killing at least four people, including two children.
item.dc:subject: News

item.title: Where are US forces stationed in Europe?
item.description: The United States operates various bases across the European continent. DW breaks down what these sites are used for, and how many troops are stationed there.
item.dc:subject: World

item.title: The wind boom Trump couldn't stop
item.description: Even with fierce opposition, offshore wind projects are multiplying along US coasts — signaling a massive shift in energy.
item.dc:subject: Environment

item.title: Unseasonal May heat wave grips Europe
item.description: The UK and France have registered record May temperatures as western Europe swelters under a "heat dome." Such extremes are occurring more often as the planet warms.
item.dc:subject: News

item.title: Berlin Hauptbahnhof at 20: A rail hub with symbolic power
item.description: Berlin's central station was inaugurated in May 2006. Beyond its functionality, the modern glass-and-steel structure was built to play a key role in reunified Germany.
item.dc:subject: Culture

item.title: Will SpaceX's IPO fund life on Mars — and a trillionaire?
item.description: SpaceX is launching history's biggest public listing, propelling Elon Musk's wealth into the stratosphere. DW explores whether the firm's out-of-this-world ambitions are truly cosmic or another black hole for investors.
item.dc:subject: Business

item.title: Germany news: More than 1,900 missing children
item.description: Authorities say there are 1,933 missing children cases nationwide, up 6.8% from the year before. And, travellers in Germany seem nervous when it comes to air travel, amid the war in Iran, a survey suggests.
item.dc:subject: News

item.title: Germany's Dr. Wolff cosmetics company is going all in on AI
item.description: This medium-sized, family-run business has embraced artificial intelligence to stay competitive. All employees are encouraged to learn about and apply LLMs.
item.dc:subject: Germany

item.title: Blinded by the light pollution: Cities seek to restore night
item.description: Artificial lights are blotting out the moon and stars, harming human health, and disrupting the natural world. Why is this a problem? And how can we stop it without sitting in the dark?
item.dc:subject: Environment

item.title: Why are Europe's biggest defense projects in trouble?
item.description: Disputes over leadership, military priorities and industrial control are threatening to fracture Europe's €100 billion fighter jet project — with tensions now spilling into a Franco-German tank project as well.
item.dc:subject: World

item.title: Madrid protesters decry rising rents and housing shortage
item.description: Housing has long ranked among the top concerns in Spain, where demand has risen because of tourism and immigration-driven population growth.
item.dc:subject: News

item.title: Germany plans to address growing use of date-rape drugs
item.description: Germany's justice minister is seeking harsher penalties for the use of date-rape drugs in sexual assaults. But victims say more needs to be done about a growing problem.
item.dc:subject: Germany

item.title: Displaced Ukrainians risk home seizure under Russian law
item.description: Russia is tightening control over property in occupied Ukrainian territories, requiring owners to register under Russian law. Many Ukrainians say returning to do so would put their safety and freedom at risk.
item.dc:subject: World

item.title: Enhanced Games: Why 'crippling injuries' are the least of it
item.description: Doping in sports is nothing new. Athletes at the Enhanced Games will know the risks — severe injury, even paralysis. But bioethicists are also concerned we're opening the gates to altering our fundamental human biology.
item.dc:subject: Science

item.title: Serbia: Police clash with protesters urging early elections
item.description: Officers fired teargas and stun grenades as tens of thousands rallied against President Aleksandar Vucic's government. The demonstration went ahead despite the state rail operator canceling trains to and from Belgrade.
item.dc:subject: News

item.title: Cyprus election expected to shake up national politics
item.description: Cyprus goes to the polls to elect a new parliament on Sunday. The vote is expected to reshape the country's political landscape, weaken traditional parties and accelerate the rise of new anti-establishment movements.
item.dc:subject: World

item.title: What is Pentecost?
item.description: People new to Germany are often surprised by the number of public holidays in the spring. Pentecost is one of them, but its significance is not well-known.
item.dc:subject: Culture

item.title: Germany: Record numbers take German passport in 2025
item.description: A record number of people received a German passport in 2025, a media report says. Numbers have been rising since Germany changed citizenship requirements in 2024.
item.dc:subject: News

item.title: Czechia: Uproar over Sudeten German gathering in Brno
item.description: The first gathering of Sudeten Germans in Czechia since World War II is happening this weekend, despite a political backlash that has exposed tensions over one of Central Europe's most painful historical legacies.
item.dc:subject: World

item.title: Germany's 'rapid militarization' threatens basic rights
item.description: Ten human rights organizations say security interests are increasingly being prioritized over fundamental rights in Germany. The scathing criticism was presented in their "2026 Fundamental Rights Report."
item.dc:subject: Germany

item.title: Kevin Warsh: Trump ally at the Federal Reserve?
item.description: Never before has a chair of the US Federal Reserve been so controversial. Who is Kevin Warsh? Can he preserve the Fed's independence, or will he become Donald Trump's puppet?
item.dc:subject: Business

item.title: US or Iran: Who will win the Hormuz endurance game?
item.description: Tehran is betting that its sanctions‑hardened economy can outlast the Strait of Hormuz crisis. Washington, meanwhile, is contending with elevated oil prices, renewed inflation pressure and rising global recession risks.
item.dc:subject: Business

item.title: The dying dream of the dead to be at peace with life
item.description: Many people have vivid dreams shortly before death. Research suggests the dreams are not a sign of confusion — but may help both the dying and their loved ones make sense of loss.
item.dc:subject: Science

item.title: What happened to jailed Belarus dissident Raman Pratasevich?
item.description: In 2021, Belarus diverted a Ryanair plane to seize opposition journalist Raman Pratasevich. What has happened to him since, and why is he now seemingly supporting the Belarusian regime?
item.dc:subject: World

item.title: How the Cannes 2026 films reflect a world in conflict
item.description: Various works explore the impact of authoritarianism through a historical lens, while one thriller on corruption is set in Putin's Russia.
item.dc:subject: Culture

item.title: DW exclusive: Germany's far-right AfD vows to 'make history'
item.description: The AfD looks set to take power in a German state for the first time. In a DW exclusive, the front-runner in Saxony-Anhalt discusses plans to detain immigrants and revamp the school system, and relations with Russia.
item.dc:subject: Germany

item.title: Ebola patient in Berlin: Why Germany's helping
item.description: An Ebola‑infected US doctor is being treated under the highest safety standards in a specialized isolation unit in the German capital, Berlin. The facilities are considered to be among the best equipped worldwide.
item.dc:subject: Science

item.title: Will Germany's aviation tax cut lower ticket prices?
item.description: The tax cut aims to ease the burden on the aviation industry and make flying slightly cheaper. However, some experts warn that it is unlikely to have a meaningful impact on ticket prices.
item.dc:subject: Business

item.title: Germany reaches out to Gulf states over Iran war
item.description: Germany no longer believes it can play a mediating role in the conflict between the US, Israel and Iran. Instead, the German government is working to strengthen its ties with the Gulf states.
item.dc:subject: Germany

item.title: Turkey: DW's Alican Uludag released, but trial continues
item.description: A Turkish court has allowed DW reporter Alican Uludag to leave detention as his contentious trial opens. He is accused of having "insulted" President Recep Tayyip Erdogan, which in Turkey can carry a jail sentence.
item.dc:subject: News

item.title: Germany 2026 World Cup squad announcement: Neuer returns
item.description: Two years after retiring from international football, Manuel Neuer is back in Germany's 2026 World Cup squad.
item.dc:subject: Sports

item.title: Extreme climate scenario fades, but warming continues
item.description: The rapid rollout of renewable energy is helping to shift emissions trends, but expected temperature rises remain high as the UN moves to tighten countries' commitments.
item.dc:subject: Environment

item.title: Freiburg's Europa League final proof another way can work
item.description: Bundesliga side Freiburg might have lost their first-ever European final to Aston Villa, but their European run is a reminder that success in football can be achieved without big spending or a loss of identity.
item.dc:subject: Sports

item.title: Germany records high numbers of online child abuse cases
item.description: Efforts to regulate social media have had little effect so far, according to Jugendschutz.net. The government-funded organization says young people in Germany are defenseless against online hate and violence.
item.dc:subject: Germany

item.title: German authorities arrest 2 on China high-tech espionage allegations
item.description: Two Munich residents have been arrested on suspicion of working for a Chinese intelligence service, seeking to obtain and pass on high-tech information with "military applications."
item.dc:subject: News

item.title: Why Stephen Colbert's 'Late Show' mattered
item.description: As the final curtain falls on "The Late Show," a brief history of the evolution of the late-night tradition and Stephen Colbert's satire.
item.dc:subject: Culture

item.title: Researchers spot humpback whales off Australia and Brazil — longest lifetime journey on record
item.description: Researchers have observed two humpback whales swimming between Australia and Brazil. It's the longest travel distance humans have ever recorded for the species.
item.dc:subject: News

item.title: How Meta wants to profile 13-year-olds on Insta, Facebook
item.description: Instagram and Facebook have a problem with underage users — there's too many of them. Parent company, Meta, aims to root out under-13s with an age-profiling AI. This plays into a debate about a teenage social media ban.
item.dc:subject: Science

item.title: EU is failing to cut pesticide use
item.description: With binding cut targets scrapped, the European Union's pledge to halve pesticide use is stalling — even as controversial chemicals like glyphosate remain on sale across the bloc.
item.dc:subject: Environment

item.title: Enhanced Games: Pushing human potential, or lethal circus?
item.description: Investors like Peter Thiel and Donald Trump Jr. see the Enhanced Games as proof of how far human limits can stretch. For doping experts, the danger is clear and grave. DW explains the latest controversial sporting event.
item.dc:subject: Sports

item.title: Pioneer of pop music: Cher turns 80
item.description: Cher has had countless hits and sparked more than a few controversies — while always doing things her own way. At 80, she remains as iconic as ever.
item.dc:subject: NRS-Import

item.title: Germany beefs up civil defense
item.description: Germany's government wants to spend €10 billion to better protect its population in case of emergencies. The backlog is huge.
item.dc:subject: Germany

item.title: Hearing aids may cut noise by reading brain signals
item.description: In a crowded room, it can be impossible for hearing aid users to focus on what they want or need to hear. Research into auditory neurotechnology may enhance sounds and voices and reduce noise in real-time.
item.dc:subject: Science

item.title: How China became Russia's economic lifeline
item.description: Hot on the heels of Donald Trump's visit, Russian President Vladimir Putin travels to Beijing for talks with Xi Jinping. DW explores how China's trade and financial support is helping keep Russia's economy afloat.
item.dc:subject: Business

item.title: Germany news: 2030 climate goals in danger, experts warn
item.description: Headlines in Germany this Monday include higher than planned CO2 emissions, plans to ramp up civil defense measures, and Chancellor Merz meeting euroskeptic Bulgarian Premier Rumen Radev.
item.dc:subject: News

item.title: Germany's crisis-hit chemical industry seeks revival
item.description: Hit by high energy costs and regulation, Germany's chemical companies are scaling back production at home while expanding abroad. Can the trend be reversed?
item.dc:subject: Business

item.title: Elversberg: Bundesliga newcomers from tiny town make history
item.description: A team from a small southwestern German town of just 7,000 inhabitants has made history by sealing promotion to become the 59th club to play in the Bundesliga.
item.dc:subject: Sports

item.title: Ebola in Africa: Why it's a constant threat
item.description: Ebola is highly contagious and deadly. It spreads internationally, such as in the May 2026 outbreaks in the Democratic Republic of the Congo and Uganda. But that doesn't automatically make it a pandemic.
item.dc:subject: Science

item.title: German singer Heino pushes back against the far-right AfD
item.description: A far‑right politician in Germany used schlager icon Heino in election advertising without his consent. Heino is now demanding compensation for pain and suffering.
item.dc:subject: Germany

item.title: Germany and Japan test hydrogen future with BMW, Toyota cars
item.description: During a recent visit to Japan, Germany's transport minister Patrick Schnieder toured hydrogen projects aimed at bringing the fuel into the mainstream.
item.dc:subject: Business

item.title: 'From collection to connection': How museums are redefining themselves
item.description: Gone are the days when museums were just places to see art and artifacts. On International Museum Day, DW looks at how museums are becoming unique spaces of community engagement and participation.
item.dc:subject: Culture

item.title: Andreas Rettig: 'You can't separate sports and politics'
item.description: The World Cup in the USA, Canada and Mexico is sparking discussions beyond the realm of football. DFB Director Andreas Rettig discusses the Germany team and the geopolitical situation in an interview with DW.
item.dc:subject: Sports

item.title: Iconic German musician Udo Lindenberg turns 80
item.description: From a historic East German concert to a hit with rapper Apache 207, Udo Lindenberg has shaped German rock music like few others. Even at 80, the "Panikrocker" is still reinventing himself.
item.dc:subject: Culture

item.title: Asteroid 2026 JH2 — no need to worry about it hitting Earth
item.description: This big asteroid was discovered shortly before it was expected to hurtle past Earth. Any time you see a headline about asteroids approaching the planet, you can relax. Big and small asteroids fly by all the time.
item.dc:subject: Science

item.title: 'FIFA got greedy' ― Why football fans in India may not be able to watch World Cup games on TV this year
item.description: The 2026 World Cup is weeks away, but one of the most populated countries in the world doesn't have a broadcasting deal. Why?
item.dc:subject: Sports

item.title: Lab-grown Tyrannosaurus leather: More chicken than dinosaur?
item.description: A handbag is promoted as the "world's first T. rex leather product". But scientists are questioning its authenticity.
item.dc:subject: Culture

item.title: Who is Mario Draghi, this year's Charlemagne Prize winner?
item.description: The Charlemagne Prize honors those who advance European unity. This year's winner is Italy's Mario Draghi, a former European Central Bank chief and a key architect of European stability during a time of economic crisis.
item.dc:subject: World

item.title: Honda reports first loss since 1957 as it waters down EV strategy, but shares rise on 2026 forecasts
item.description: The Japanese carmaker pursued an aggressive switch towards electric vehicles that it's now trying to walk back. Despite posting its first loss since 1957, its share price spiked on more buoyant predictions for 2026.
item.dc:subject: News

item.title: China goes electric, but can it get off coal?
item.description: The world's clean energy superpower also emits the most CO2. But coal-fired power may have peaked as China electrifies its economy with ever more renewables.
item.dc:subject: Environment

item.title: US drops bond requirement for FIFA World Cup ticket holders
item.description: Fans from five African World Cup qualifying nations with valid tickets are exempt from paying a hefty bond. But fans from several other countries still face major hurdles to seeing their teams play in the United States.
item.dc:subject: News

item.title: Fake TikTok fans and pop music's 'unethical' marketing
item.description: Do indie darlings like Sombr and Geese owe their success to TikTok "trend simulations"? The question has sent shockwaves through their fan base. Yet manipulated charts are as old as the music business itself.
item.dc:subject: Culture

item.title: China’s new rules give the West a new headache
item.description: Beijing now has strong new powers to punish firms for pulling manufacturing out of China. Multinationals are increasingly caught in a web of coercion and complex rules as they try to operate across the US, EU and China.
item.dc:subject: Business

item.title: Satellites add a new layer to global poverty data
item.description: A new study using satellite data finds that 58% of the global population has been assigned to the wrong tier in the UN's Human Development Index. That can have real-world consequences for how aid reaches people.
item.dc:subject: Science

item.title: Why men are less worried than women about climate change
item.description: From petro-masculinity to electric trucks: why men disengage from climate action and what might bring them back.
item.dc:subject: Environment

item.title: Hotter 2026 and El Nino could trigger extreme fires
item.description: More than 150 million hectares — over twice the size of Texas — burned globally in the first months of 2026. With a high chance of a supercharged El Nino, the second half of the year could be even worse.
item.dc:subject: Environment

item.title: Bundesliga: Kathleen Krüger appointed Hamburg's head of sport
item.description: Hamburg have appointed Kathleen Krüger as the club's new board member responsible for sport. She becomes the first woman to fill such a role at a Bundesliga club on a permanent basis.
item.dc:subject: Sports

item.title: Sport helping displaced Lebanese living in stadium to cope
item.description: More than a million people have been forced to flee their homes in southern Lebanon due to fighting between Israeli forces and Hezbollah. As many shelter in Beirut stadiums, sport is sometimes a welcome distraction.
item.dc:subject: Sports

item.title: Hijab-wearing German beauty contestant takes on the AfD
item.description: As a Miss Germany contestant, Büsra Sayed wore a hijab on stage. This had never happened before — and the far-right AfD didn't like it. Her response went viral.
item.dc:subject: Germany

item.title: Hantavirus: How is the outbreak being contained?
item.description: Passengers evacuated from the MV Hondius cruise ship are returning to their home countries, some with symptoms, many without. What happens to these people and what is being done to contain the spread?
item.dc:subject: Science

item.title: Architecture of remembrance and hope: Daniel Libeskind at 80
item.description: Famous for designing the Jewish Museum Berlin and the Ground Zero project in New York, the architect has created dozens of memorable buildings worldwide.
item.dc:subject: Culture

item.title: Hantavirus: How it differs from COVID
item.description: In 2018-2019, the Andes virus hantavirus spread person-to-person in Argentina. Eleven people died — but it was stopped. Analysis of that outbreak shows the current one can also be stopped.
item.dc:subject: Science

item.title: Endemic, epidemic, pandemic: What's the difference?
item.description: The Andes hantavirus outbreak shows how easily disease labels are misunderstood — and why they describe patterns of spread, not how dangerous a virus is.
item.dc:subject: Science

item.title: Somali piracy disrupts global shipping and trade routes
item.description: As ships reroute around Africa to avoid Middle East conflict zones, piracy off Somalia is back. The resurgence is driving up insurance, transit times and security costs, putting new pressure on global supply chains.
item.dc:subject: Business

item.title: Why is Hollywood missing from Cannes?
item.description: There are no US blockbusters premiering at the Cannes Film Festival this year. So who's snubbing who?
item.dc:subject: Culture

item.title: New film portrays Thomas and Erika Mann — with an oddly twisted premise
item.description: Premiering at Cannes and starring Sandra Hüller, Pawel Pawlikowski's "Fatherland" is set during the Cold War — a complex period for the prominent anti-Nazi intellectuals.
item.dc:subject: Culture

item.title: Eurovision 2026: Politics collide with a blockbuster show
item.description: Controversy over Israel's participation and boycotts unprecedented in scale are already weighing on the Eurovision Song Contest before it even kicks off in Vienna.
item.dc:subject: Culture

item.title: Celebrated broadcaster and naturalist David Attenborough turns 100
item.description: The still-sprightly centenarian has been knighted twice, nominated for the 2022 Nobel Peace Prize, and is perhaps the world's most recognizable nature lover.
item.dc:subject: Culture

item.title: World Cup 'prices will drop' but too late for traveling fans
item.description: Among many firsts at World Cup 2026, FIFA has tried its hand at dynamic pricing and acting as a secondary seller. The tactic has seen a ticket listed for over $2 million, but many games seem undersold. What's going on?
item.dc:subject: Sports

item.title: Why the EU sees Chinese solar tech as a major security risk
item.description: Europe's solar boom is powered by Chinese technology. But experts warn Chinese-made tech could threaten the continent's safety and even create blackouts. Now, Brussels is aiming to reduce its dependence.
item.dc:subject: Business

item.title: Bayern Munich exit Champions League in semifinal to PSG
item.description: Holders Paris Saint-Germain are one game away from retaining the Champions League after knocking out German champions Bayern Munich. Bayern can still complete the domestic double but again miss out on the big one.
item.dc:subject: Sports

item.title: COVID pioneer BioNTech: The fall of Germany's pandemic hero
item.description: BioNTech, once celebrated for its COVID-19 vaccines, is grappling with falling demand, restructuring and the departure of its founders. Can its ambitious cancer trials turn it from a one-hit wonder into a biotech giant?
item.dc:subject: Business

item.title: Venice Biennale in crisis: The controversies explained
item.description: Following the resignation of the jury over Russia's and Israel's disputed participation, the traditional Golden Lion will not be awarded this year.
item.dc:subject: Culture

item.title: Hungary's new government pushes for euro by 2030
item.description: Hungary's new leadership wants the country to adopt the euro by the end of the decade while repairing strained ties with Brussels. But with a weak economy and tight deadlines, experts warn the path will be steep.
item.dc:subject: Business

item.title: What the Strait of Hormuz fuel crisis means for air travel
item.description: Does travel insurance cover flight cancellations caused by the Iran war? Travelers could face major disruption as the jet fuel crisis threatens summer flights.
item.dc:subject: Culture

item.title: How Rosalia became a global pop force
item.description: From a small town in Spain to sold-out global arenas, Spanish singer Rosalia has built her career with precision rather than luck. How did she do it?
item.dc:subject: Culture

item.title: What is hantavirus?
item.description: Three people have died on a cruise ship in the Atlantic, reportedly from hantavirus. The dangerous Andes strain has been confirmed in two cases. What are the symptoms, and how is it transmitted?
item.dc:subject: Science

item.title: The sea that is vanishing in real time
item.description: The world's largest inland body of water is retreating at an alarming speed. From stranded buildings to vanishing habitats, scientists warn the Caspian Sea may be approaching a tipping point.
item.dc:subject: Environment

item.title: Dubai's image as a financial hub faces its biggest test yet
item.description: Amid the smoldering Iran war, wealthy elites are quietly shifting capital and other assets out of Dubai and heading to Singapore and Switzerland. Just how resilient is the emirate's safe-haven reputation?
item.dc:subject: Business

item.title: Why Saudi Arabia is withdrawing from sport
item.description: There are few major sports left that are not supported by investment from Saudi Arabia. But with the kingdom pulling out of golf and canceling planned events and contracts elsewhere, is the Saudi sports era over?
item.dc:subject: Sports

item.title: Women's Champions League: Bayern fall to Barcelona in semis
item.description: Bayern Munich have come up short in their bid to win the Women's Champions League for the first time after losing to Barcelona in the semifinals.
item.dc:subject: Sports

item.title: Alex Zanardi, F1 driver and multiple CART and Paralympic champion, dies at 59
item.description: Italian race driver turned Paralympian Alex Zanardi has died aged 59. The former Formula 1 driver and two-time CART champion lost his legs in a horrific crash at the peak of his powers, but his drive to compete endured.
item.dc:subject: News

item.title: Gender investment gap: Are women catching up with men?
item.description: Online brokers are tapping into the potential of female investors. Tailor-made products and branding campaigns are on the rise. Will this help bridge the gap between women and men when it comes to investing?
item.dc:subject: Business
